summary: Peter I "the Great" (r. 1682–1725) was a modernizing, Westernizing tsar of Russia who won the Great Northern War against Charles XII of Sweden (decisively at the Battle of Poltava), founded St. Petersburg as a "window to the West" on the Baltic, and became the first Russian ruler to call himself Emperor. Famous for beard-hating reforms — a beard tax on the boyars and the meritocratic Table of Ranks — he toured Western Europe incognito on his 1697–98 "Grand Embassy," working as a shipbuilder in the Netherlands, and later crushed multiple streltsy revolts tied to his half-sister Sophia.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Peter the Great is identified in giveaways as the modernizing, Westernizing tsar (later the first Emperor) of Russia who defeated Charles XII of Sweden to win the Great Northern War. Clues also note that "Peter" was shared by three Romanov monarchs, of whom he is by far the most famous, and that he personally commissioned Vitus Bering's Great Northern Expedition to map the Siberian coast and Kamchatka.

clues.clue: Modernizing/Westernizing tsar of Russia who defeated Charles XII of Sweden to win the Great Northern War
examples: name this modernizing tsar of Russia who won the Great Northern War.
name this westernizing ruler who excised a beard tax on the boyars and defeated Charles XII of Sweden in the Great Northern War.

clues.clue: Became the first Russian ruler to call himself Emperor, enacting sweeping reforms
examples: name this tsar who made sweeping reforms as the first Emperor of Russia.
He was the first tsar to call himself emperor and led Russia to victory in the Great Northern War.

clues.clue: The name "Peter" (Pyotr) was shared by three Romanov monarchs of Russia, of whom he is the most famous, nicknamed "the Great"
examples: give this name shared by three Romanov monarchs of Russia, including a moderniser nicknamed "the Great".

clues.clue: Commissioned Vitus Bering's Great Northern Expedition to Kamchatka, which mapped much of the Siberian coast
examples: Peter the Great commissioned this man's expedition to Kamchatka. This leader of the Great Northern Expedition mapped much of the Siberian coast.

works.name: Streltsy Revolts & the Regency of Sophia
works.description: The first years of Peter's reign were consumed by a power struggle between his father's two wives' families, the Miloslavskys and the Naryshkins, which his half-sister Sophia exploited to incite a streltsy uprising; a second streltsy revolt broke out in 1698 while Peter was abroad on his Grand Embassy, again aiming to restore Sophia, and he crushed it brutally on his return before eventually disbanding the streltsy altogether.

clues.clue: Early power struggle between the Miloslavsky and Naryshkin families (his father's two wives' relatives) let his half-sister Sophia incite a streltsy uprising, alongside Vasily Galitzine
examples: The first years of this ruler's reign saw a power struggle between the families of his father's wives, the Miloslavskys and the Naryshkins.
He spent his early years in a power struggle with Vasily Galitzine and his half-sister Sophia.

clues.clue: In youth, gained military experience through war games with a "toy army," including exercises at the wooden fortress "Presburg" helped by Franz Zimmerman
examples: Franz Zimmerman helped this ruler stage military exercises at a wooden fortress named "Presburg."
In his youth, this man gained military experience in war games he played with his "toy army."

clues.clue: A 1698 streltsy revolt broke out while he was abroad on his Grand Embassy, aiming to replace him with Sophia; he cut the trip short and executed the rebels on his return
examples: This ruler quelled a streltsy revolt aiming to replace him with his sister Sofia after returning from his Grand Embassy.
This ruler executed soldiers who attempted to replace him with Sophia Alekseyevna after cutting short his "Grand Embassy."

clues.clue: Eventually disbanded the streltsy entirely after the repeated revolts
examples: Following several revolts, he disbanded the streltsy.

clues.clue: An earlier revolt against him was put down with help from the Scotsman Patrick Gordon, whose "moveable ramparts" innovation later also helped seize Azov
examples: A revolt against this ruler was put down with the help of Scotsman Patrick Gordon, whose innovation of "moveable ramparts" helped this ruler seize Azov.

works.name: The Grand Embassy (1697–98)
works.description: Peter's 1697–98 "Grand Embassy" sent him touring Western Europe incognito under the alias "Pyotr Mikhailov" to learn Western customs and forge an anti-Ottoman alliance; he personally worked as a shipbuilder in the Netherlands, stayed (and caused chaos) at an English estate, and returned home having decided to Westernize his court, only for the trip to be cut short by a streltsy revolt.

clues.clue: Traveled Western Europe in disguise, adopting the alias "Pyotr Mikhailov" to conceal his identity
examples: This man adopted the surname "Mikhailov" to disguise his identity during a tour of Europe that strived to forge alliances against the Ottoman Empire.
This policy was adopted after a ruler took a trip disguised as Pyotr Mikhailov ("mee-ha-eel-ov").

clues.clue: Worked incognito as a shipbuilder in the Netherlands (Zaandam and Amsterdam), staying with his old friend Gerrit Kist and working for Lynst Rogge; the small house he stayed in at Zaandam now bears his name
examples: The leader of this event worked as a shipbuilder in a small house in Zaandam that now bears his name.
This ruler found his old friend Gerrit Kist in Zaandam and worked for Lynst Rogge at a shipyard while part of the Grand Embassy.

clues.clue: Publicly/nominally led by his Swiss-born general Franz Lefort, who was named one of three "Grand Ambassadors"; other members included Alexander Menshikov
examples: Publicly led by Franz Lefort, this event failed to gather support for a crusade against the Ottoman Empire.
This ruler named Franz Lefort as one of three "Grand Ambassadors."

clues.clue: Sought but failed to build a military alliance against the Ottoman Empire, despite appeals to William III of England and Holy Roman Emperor Leopold I
examples: This group failed to achieve its objective of building an anti-Ottoman alliance despite appeals to William III of England and Holy Roman Emperor Leopold I.
This event failed to gather support for a crusade against the Ottoman Empire.

clues.clue: Stayed at Sayes Court, an English estate lent by William III and belonging to the diarist John Evelyn, and trashed the place (ruining its hedgerows) during his stay
examples: This man was provided Sayes Court by William III of England, but then trashed the place during his stay.
This person ruined the hedgerows of Sayes Court when the diarist John Evelyn took him in as a guest.

clues.clue: Astronomer Edmund Halley reportedly pushed him around a garden in a wheelbarrow
examples: Edmund Halley supposedly pushed this group's leader around a garden in a wheelbarrow.

clues.clue: Had an affair with the British actress Letitia Cross during the trip
examples: An affair with the British actress Letitia Cross began during this event, which was nominally organized by Franz Lefort.

clues.clue: Observing foreign civil-service structures during the trip helped inspire his later Table of Ranks
examples: Civil services observed during this event inspired the Table of Ranks.

clues.clue: Employed advanced foreign specialists recruited on the trip, including Dr. Nikolaas Bidloo, Dr. Robert Erskine, the engineer John Perry, and the Dutch naval commander Cornelis Cruys
examples: This event led to the employment of such advanced scientists as Dr. Nikolaas Bidloo, Dr. Robert Erskine, and the engineer John Perry.
The employment of Dutch naval commander Cornelis Cruys was secured by this group.

clues.clue: Met the collector Jacob de Wilde, an encounter depicted in an engraving by de Wilde's daughter Maria de Wilde
examples: Maria de Wilde created an engraving that depicts this man meeting with Maria's father, the collector Jacob de Wilde.

clues.clue: A conspiracy theory held that during the trip he was tarred and stuffed in a barrel (or captured and held in Riga) and replaced at home by a German imposter
examples: A conspiracy theory held that, during this event, the ruler who began it was tarred and stuffed in a barrel or captured and held in Riga, and was replaced at home by a German imposter.

clues.clue: At a dance during the trip, women wearing whalebone corsets led onlookers to hypothesize their rib cages were on the outside of their bodies
examples: In one incident from this event, visitors dancing with women who wore whalebone corsets hypothesized that those women's rib cages were on the outside of their body.

works.name: Azov Campaigns & Naval Buildup
works.description: Fighting alongside Austria, Venice, and Poland in the Holy League, Peter captured the Ottoman fortress of Azov (aided by Patrick Gordon's "moveable ramparts"), then founded a new fortress base at Taganrog and the naval base of Kronstadt to secure his gains and build a fleet.

clues.clue: Captured the Ottoman fortress of Azov, fighting alongside Austria, Venice, and Poland in the Holy League
examples: This ruler expanded his territory in his Azov campaign against the Ottomans, after which he founded a base at Taganrog.
founded the city of Taganrog after fighting alongside Austria, Venice, and Poland in the Holy League and capturing Azov.

clues.clue: Founded a fortress base at Taganrog after the successful Azov campaigns
examples: This ruler built a fortress at Taganrog after his successful campaigns in Azov.
This person built Taganrog as a base for his fleet, which was commanded by the Scottish mercenary Patrick Gordon.

clues.clue: Alexander Menshikov led his "toy army" during the Azov campaigns
examples: during which Alexander Menshikov led his "toy army."

clues.clue: Built a new naval base at Kronstadt; ships built there were later used in his navy's victory at the Battle of Gangut
examples: Ships built at the newly-constructed base of Kronstadt were used in this ruler's victory at the Battle of Gangut.

works.name: The Great Northern War
works.description: Peter's decades-long war against Charles XII of Sweden, allied with Augustus the Strong of Saxony-Poland, culminated in his decisive land victory at the Battle of Poltava and naval victory at the Battle of Gangut, winning Russia a Baltic coastline and ending in the Treaty of Nystad.

clues.clue: Defeated Charles XII of Sweden to win the Great Northern War, gaining Russia parts of the Baltic coast
examples: The victor at the Battle of Poltava, name this ruler who defeated Charles XII of Sweden in the Great Northern War.
After he returned home, he modernized his country by requiring that boyars shave off their beards and acquiring parts of the Baltic coast from Charles XII.

clues.clue: Won the decisive Battle of Poltava
examples: The victor at the Battle of Poltava name this ruler who defeated Charles XII of Sweden in the Great Northern War.
Name this Russian leader who destroyed the rebellious streltsy and laid plans for the Russian victory at the Battle of Poltava.

clues.clue: Won a naval victory at the Battle of Gangut
examples: this ruler's navy won the Battle of Gangut.
Ships built at the newly-constructed base of Kronstadt were used in this ruler's victory at the Battle of Gangut.

clues.clue: Ordered 70 galleys to run a Swedish naval blockade begun by Admiral Gustaf Wattrang near the Aland Islands
examples: To relieve a blockade begun by Admiral Gustaf Wattrang, this ruler ordered 70 galleys to run the blockade near the Aland Islands.

clues.clue: His army forced an opposing Swedish force to undertake the Carolean Death March
examples: This ruler's army caused an opposing force to undertake the Carolean Death March.

clues.clue: Allied with Augustus the Strong of Saxony-Poland against Charles XII, meeting him at the Birzai Conference
examples: Augustus supported this czar during the Great Northern War, which this man fought against Charles XII.
This ruler entrusted several diplomatic assignments to his Swiss-born general, Franz Lefort, and held the Birzai Conference with one of his allies.

clues.clue: His foreign policy, influenced by the German adventurer Johann Patkul, led to the war-ending Treaty of Nystad
examples: This man's foreign policy was influenced by Johann Patkul, a German adventurer, eventually leading to the Treaty of Nystad.

clues.clue: Waged a "Persian campaign" against Persia, remembered as the second of the Russo-Persian Wars
examples: The second Russo-Persian War was known as the "Persian campaign" of this Russian ruler.

works.name: State & Bureaucratic Reforms: the Table of Ranks
works.description: In 1722 Peter created the Table of Ranks, a fourteen-level meritocratic hierarchy for military, civil, and court service that replaced the old heredity-based mestnichestvo system and let commoners earn hereditary nobility; he paired it with consolidating government departments into the Twelve Collegia, a new watchdog "Ober-procurator" post, and sweeping fiscal and land reforms.

clues.clue: Created the Table of Ranks (1722), a fourteen-rank meritocratic hierarchy for military and civil service replacing the hereditary mestnichestvo system
examples: This tsar replaced the existing mestnichestvo system by creating a fourteen-rank hierarchy for military and civil services called the Table of Ranks.
In 1722, Peter the Great introduced this system, which created a meritocratic hierarchy of 14 categories across military and civil services.

clues.clue: Under the Table of Ranks, reaching the eighth level granted a commoner hereditary nobility
examples: Anyone who reached the eighth level was given hereditary nobility.

clues.clue: Consolidated the growing number of prikazy (executive departments) into twelve executive bodies, the Collegia
examples: This ruler consolidated a growing number of prikazy into twelve executive bodies called collegia.
This ruler established the Twelve Collegia.

clues.clue: Created the "Ober-procurator"/General Procurer position, the "sovereign's eye" overseeing the Senate and the corruption-hunting fiskaly
examples: This ruler created a special position called the Ober-procurator intended to act as the "sovereign's eye" between him and the Senate.
He imposed heavy fines on senators who missed their duties or talked idly while senate was in progress, and established the General Procurer position to oversee both the senate and the fiskaly.

clues.clue: Declaring "money is the artery of war," replaced the estate tax with a poll tax of 70 kopeks per person to raise revenue
examples: This ruler, who declared that "money is the artery of war," increased his revenue by replacing an estate tax with a poll tax of 70 kopeks per person.

clues.clue: Eliminated the distinction between hereditary and granted lands and mandated primogeniture
examples: He eliminated the distinction between hereditary and granted lands, and he mandated primogeniture.

clues.clue: Massively industrialized Russia without relying on foreign capital
examples: Also, he massively industrialized his nation without the use of foreign capital.

clues.clue: The Table of Ranks was later undercut by Empress Anna of Russia's 1731 decrees privileging military over civil ranks
examples: This Russian empress from Courland ruined Peter's system by issuing two 1731 decrees that put military ranks higher in precedence than their civil counterparts.

works.name: The Beard Tax
works.description: Peter imposed a tax on beards to force the boyars to adopt a Westernized appearance, inspired partly by the clean-shaven fashions of Louis XIV's French court; those who paid received a token (stamped with an eagle and a face) proving compliance, and men caught without one could have their beards publicly shaved, drawing mockery from satirical luboks and condemnation from the Russian Orthodox Church.

clues.clue: Imposed a beard tax on the boyars to force a Westernized appearance
examples: This ruler learned shipbuilding in the Netherlands as part of his "Grand Embassy," and he attempted to westernize his country by making the boyars shave their beards.
The tax on beards was introduced by this reformist tsar, who wanted the boyars to look more westernized.

clues.clue: Those who paid the tax received a token stamped with an eagle on one side and a lower face on the other, inscribed as a "superfluous burden"; men without one risked having their beard publicly shaved
examples: Under this ruler, those without a token indicating tax compliance ran the risk of having their beard publicly shaved.
People who had one of these things had to carry a token that depicted an eagle on one side and a lower part of face on the other.

clues.clue: Peasants and clergy were exempt; the tax targeted the nobility specifically
examples: Peasants and clergy were exempted from the levy on these items, which was aimed at the nobility.

clues.clue: Was inspired to require shaving by the fashions of Louis XIV's French court
examples: Peter the Great was inspired to require people to shave by the court of this French "Sun King."

clues.clue: Old Believers, who had split from the Russian Orthodox Church over Patriarch Nikon's liturgical reforms, mainly paid a special "znak" token to keep their beards
examples: Members of this other religious group mainly paid for a znak, or token, to maintain their beards.

clues.clue: The policy was mocked in a satirical lubok (folk print), attributed to Vasili Loren, depicting a cat that "farts sweetly"
examples: This policy was parodied by a lubok depicting a cat that "farts sweetly," which is often attributed to Vasili Loren.

works.name: Church Reforms
works.description: Peter subordinated the Russian Orthodox Church to the state, replacing the Patriarchate with the Most Holy Synod (headed by his hand-picked Feofan Prokopovich) after Patriarch Adrian's death, while also mocking the Church by founding a parody club known as the "All-Drunken Synod of Fools and Jesters."

clues.clue: After the death of Patriarch Adrian, replaced the Patriarchate with the Most Holy Synod, ending the position of Patriarch of Moscow and All Russia
examples: After the death of Patriarch Adrian, this ruler replaced the Patriarchate with the Most Holy Synod.
Peter the Great abolished this religious institution's position of "Patriarch of Moscow and All Russia."

clues.clue: Installed his hand-picked patriarch/synod head Feofan Prokopovich
examples: This man's reforms of the church included the establishment of the Holy Synod, led by his hand-picked patriarch Feofan Propokovich.

clues.clue: Mocked the church by founding the "All-Drunken Synod of Fools and Jesters," appointing his former tutor as "Prince-Pope" and parodying the sviatki folk custom
examples: This ruler mocked his country's church by founding a club that he called the All-Drunken Synod of Fools and Jesters.
After this emperor appointed his former tutor as the "Prince-Pope" of a group of his drinking buddies, he decided to have his "Drunken Synod" parody the sviatki folk custom.

works.name: Founding of St. Petersburg
works.description: On land captured from Sweden during the Great Northern War, Peter founded St. Petersburg — named for his patron saint, designed by architects like Domenico Trezzini and Jean-Baptiste Alexandre Le Blond, and intended as a "window to the West" — which became Russia's second-largest city and, later, the capital of the Russian Empire.

clues.clue: Founded St. Petersburg on the Baltic, intended as a "window to the West," on a site captured from Sweden during the Great Northern War
examples: This ruler founded a "window on the west" on the Baltic that bears his name.
An equestrian statue of this tsar stands in Senate Square in a city that he intended as a "window to the west" after its site was captured from Sweden in the Great Northern War.

clues.clue: Named the city for his patron saint
examples: He ordered the construction of a city named for his patron saint which was dubbed a "window to the West."

clues.clue: St. Petersburg became Russia's second-largest city (after Moscow) and later the capital of the Russian Empire
examples: name this Westernizing tsar who founded Russia's second-largest city after Moscow.
St. Petersburg became the capital of the Russian Empire under this reforming Russian ruler.

clues.clue: The city's architects included Domenico Trezzini and Jean-Baptiste Alexandre Le Blond, and it was once home to the Twelve Colleges government system
examples: Important architects who designed this city include Domenic Trezzini and Jean-Baptiste Alexandre le Blond.

clues.clue: Financed the artists Andrew Matveev and John Nitkin to travel and study in Europe, just as he had done during his own Grand Embassy
examples: This ruler financed the artists Andrew Matveev and John Nitkin to travel to Europe, just as he had done in his "Grand Embassy."

works.name: Family, Court Life & Scandals
works.description: Peter's court saw a string of scandals: his son's failed attempt to flee abroad ended in torture and death at Peter's own order, a conspiracy against him ended with executions staged over an exhumed grave, an affair with a German woman became public knowledge, and he elevated an African prince, Abram Gannibal, to a position of power — whose great-grandson, Alexander Pushkin, would later write about them both.

clues.clue: His son's attempt to defect to Austria with Afrosinya ended with Peter having him tortured to death
examples: This ruler's son's attempt to defect to Austria with Afrosinya ended with that son being tortured to death by this ruler.

clues.clue: After the Zickler conspiracy against him failed, executed the plotters over his exhumed step-uncle's grave
examples: After the Zickler conspiracy against them failed, this ruler executed the culprits over their step-uncle's exhumed body.

clues.clue: Caused a scandal when his affair with the German woman Anna Mons was revealed
examples: This ruler caused a scandal when his affair with the German woman Anna Mons was revealed.

clues.clue: Elevated the African prince Abram Gannibal (given to him as a gift, per one account, by Sultan Mustafa II) to a position of power as an army engineer; Gannibal was the great-grandfather of the poet Alexander Pushkin
examples: This emperor allowed the black general Abram Gannibal, the grandfather of a famous poet, to rise to a position of power in his court.
Sultan Mustafa II gifted this ruler with an African slave, Abram Gannibal, who became one of his smartest engineers.

works.name: Peasant & Cossack Revolts
works.description: Peter's crackdowns on peasants fleeing to Russia's frontiers sparked the Bulavin Rebellion, during which a rebel captured the Bakhmut salt mines, while the urban posad of Astrakhan separately joined the unsuccessful Astrakhan Revolt against him.

clues.clue: His crackdowns on peasants fleeing to the frontier sparked the Bulavin Rebellion, during which a rebel captured the government-seized Bakhmut salt mines
examples: This ruler's crackdowns on peasants fleeing to the outskirts of the country led to the Bulavin Rebellion.
A man rebelling against this ruler captured the Bakhmut salt mines after its seizure by the government.

clues.clue: The urban posad of Astrakhan joined the unsuccessful Astrakhan Revolt against him
examples: The posad joined in the unsuccessful Astrakhan Revolt against this monarch.

works.name: Legacy: Monuments & Cultural Depictions
works.description: Peter is memorialized by two very different equestrian/standing statues in St. Petersburg and Moscow — Etienne Falconet's Bronze Horseman and a modern 322-foot colossus possibly repurposed from a rejected Columbus monument — and by the writings of Alexander Pushkin, whose own great-grandfather Abram Gannibal had served at Peter's court.

clues.clue: The Bronze Horseman, an equestrian statue by Etienne Falconet, stands in St. Petersburg's Senate Square atop a trampled snake, with its face designed by Falconet's daughter-in-law Marie-Anne Collot
examples: Marie-Anne Collot, the daughter-in-law of the primary sculptor, designed the face for a statue of this person that stands atop a trampled snake.
The Bronze Horseman is an equestrian statue of what Russian tsar, who founded the city in which it stands?

clues.clue: The Bronze Horseman's base is the "Thunder Stone," a 1,500-ton granite monolith moved on a massive bronze-sphere sledge run
examples: The Thunder Stone is the base for a statue of this person designed by Etienne Falconet.
A massive sledge run on bronze spheres was built to transport a 1500 ton granite monolith for a statue of this person.

clues.clue: A separate, modern 322-foot-tall statue — possibly repurposed from a rejected Christopher Columbus monument — depicts him standing atop a stack of ships
examples: A rejected monument to Christopher Columbus may have been repurposed for a 322 foot-tall statue of this person that depicts them standing atop a stack of ships.

clues.clue: Alexander Pushkin's unfinished novel The Negro of Peter the Great features the protagonist Ibrahim, inspired by Pushkin's own great-grandfather Abram Gannibal, who served at Peter's court
examples: Identify this unfinished work whose protagonist, Ibrahim, was inspired by the author's own great-grandfather Abram Gannibal.

clues.clue: A monument to Peter the Great plays a large role in Alexander Pushkin's poem The Bronze Horseman
examples: A monument to Peter the Great plays a large role in his poem The Bronze Horseman.

comprehensive_summary: Peter I "the Great" (b. 1672) became tsar of Russia amid a power struggle between his father's two wives' families, the Miloslavskys and the Naryshkins, which his half-sister Sophia exploited (alongside Vasily Galitzine) to incite a streltsy uprising in the early years of his reign. In youth he trained through war games with a "toy army," including exercises at the wooden fortress "Presburg" aided by Franz Zimmerman. Fighting alongside Austria, Venice, and Poland in the Holy League, Peter captured the Ottoman fortress of Azov — aided by the Scotsman Patrick Gordon's "moveable ramparts" innovation, with Alexander Menshikov leading his "toy army" in the campaign — and founded a new fortress base at Taganrog and the naval base of Kronstadt. In 1697–98 he undertook the "Grand Embassy," touring Western Europe incognito under the alias "Pyotr Mikhailov" to learn Western customs and seek an anti-Ottoman alliance (which failed despite appeals to William III of England and Holy Roman Emperor Leopold I). He personally worked as a shipbuilder in Zaandam and Amsterdam, staying with his old friend Gerrit Kist and working for Lynst Rogge; the group was publicly led by Franz Lefort and included Alexander Menshikov. In England he stayed at Sayes Court, an estate lent by William III and belonging to the diarist John Evelyn, which he ruined during his stay; astronomer Edmund Halley reportedly pushed him around a garden in a wheelbarrow, and he had an affair with the actress Letitia Cross. The trip, which recruited foreign specialists like Dr. Nikolaas Bidloo, Dr. Robert Erskine, engineer John Perry, and naval commander Cornelis Cruys, was cut short by news of a 1698 streltsy revolt again aiming to restore Sophia; Peter returned, executed the rebels, and eventually disbanded the streltsy entirely. Inspired partly by what he observed abroad, Peter set about Westernizing and modernizing Russia: he imposed a beard tax on the boyars (inspired by the fashions of Louis XIV's French court), requiring a token proving payment or risking a public shave, exempting peasants and clergy; the Russian Orthodox Church denounced the policy, Old Believers paid a special "znak" token to keep their beards, and a satirical lubok mocked it with a farting cat. In 1722 he created the Table of Ranks, a fourteen-level meritocratic hierarchy for military and civil service (letting commoners who reached the eighth rank earn hereditary nobility) that replaced the old mestnichestvo system; he paired it with consolidating government departments into the Twelve Collegia, creating the watchdog "Ober-procurator" post, replacing the estate tax with a poll tax, eliminating the distinction between hereditary and granted lands, mandating primogeniture, and industrializing Russia without foreign capital (the Table of Ranks was later undercut by Empress Anna of Russia's 1731 decrees). He also subordinated the Church, replacing the Patriarchate with the Most Holy Synod (headed by his chosen Feofan Prokopovich) after Patriarch Adrian's death, while mocking the institution with his "All-Drunken Synod of Fools and Jesters." Militarily, Peter fought Charles XII of Sweden for decades in the Great Northern War, allied with Augustus the Strong of Saxony-Poland (meeting him at the Birzai Conference), relieving a Swedish blockade near the Aland Islands and forcing an enemy retreat known as the Carolean Death March before winning the decisive Battle of Poltava and the naval Battle of Gangut; the war, ended by the Treaty of Nystad, won Russia a Baltic coastline on which Peter founded St. Petersburg — named for his patron saint, designed by architects including Domenico Trezzini and Jean-Baptiste Alexandre Le Blond, and intended as a "window to the West" — which became Russia's second-largest city and later its imperial capital; he also fought a "Persian campaign," the second of the Russo-Persian Wars. His crackdowns on fleeing peasants sparked the Bulavin Rebellion (during which a rebel seized the government-held Bakhmut salt mines), while Astrakhan's posad separately joined the failed Astrakhan Revolt. Peter's court saw further turmoil: his son's attempted defection to Austria with Afrosinya ended in the son's torture and death at Peter's order, the failed Zickler conspiracy ended with executions staged over an exhumed grave, his affair with the German Anna Mons became a public scandal, and he elevated the African prince Abram Gannibal to a position of power as an army engineer — Gannibal's great-grandson, Alexander Pushkin, later fictionalized him in the unfinished novel The Negro of Peter the Great, while Pushkin's poem The Bronze Horseman centers on a monument to Peter himself. That monument, Etienne Falconet's equestrian Bronze Horseman (its face designed by Falconet's daughter-in-law Marie-Anne Collot), stands in St. Petersburg's Senate Square atop a trampled snake and a 1,500-ton granite "Thunder Stone" base; a separate modern 322-foot statue, possibly repurposed from a rejected Christopher Columbus monument, depicts Peter standing atop a stack of ships.
recursive_suggestions: Grand Embassy of Peter the Great — has independent tossup presence (3 direct tossups: 2025 Chicago Open, 2019 EFT, 2012 Penn Bowl) distinct from the 'Peter the Great' answerline; strong candidate for its own History deep-dive page
Table of Ranks — recurring standalone bonus answerline (appears as its own Part answer in 5+ bonuses); could support a dedicated topic on Russian bureaucratic/administrative history
Beard tax — recurring standalone bonus answerline; closely tied to the Table of Ranks and Westernizing reforms
Battle of Poltava — recurring named battle within the Great Northern War; watch for independent answerline presence in future fetches
Alexander Menshikov — recurring associate with his own bonus answerline (2013 VCU Closed); distinct topic covering his rise, exile by the Dolgorukovs, and descendants
Abram Gannibal — recurring figure (Pushkin's great-grandfather); distinct from Peter the Great and from Pushkin's novel The Negro of Peter the Great about him

Peter II of Russia and Peter III of Russia — flagged ambiguity: the 2021 COOT tossup on the shared name "Peter" mixed in clues specific to these two other Romanov monarchs (Peter II tutored by Count Ostermann; Peter III's Oranienbaum ghost legend and Second Miracle of the House of Brandenburg), which were excluded from this page
St. Petersburg — the city Peter founded has its own bonus answerline (2013 Penn Bowl) with architect and governance details; candidate for a Geography/History place page
Alexander Sergeyevich Pushkin — recurring author answerline (2014 College History Bowl) via his works about Peter the Great and Gannibal; distinct Literature topic
Augustus the Strong — recurring allied monarch (2013 NASAT) during the Great Northern War; distinct topic
